title: No runtime validation of `denom` allows out-of-contract values to reach WASM
description: The `Denom` type is a TypeScript compile-time union (1|2|4|8) with no runtime enforcement. A JavaScript caller (or any cast via `as any`) can pass 0, 3, 16, or NaN directly into `_decode`. Passing an unexpected denominator to the WASM DCT decoder may trigger undefined behavior, a panic/trap, or a memory-safety violation inside the WASM sandbox. The WASM sandbox limits blast radius but a trap surfaces as an unhandled exception that can crash the Node process.

title: No size or null guard on `jpeg` input before passing to WASM
description: An empty Uint8Array or a very large buffer is passed directly to the WASM decoder without any length check. An empty buffer will likely cause an immediate WASM trap; an extremely large buffer (e.g. >2 GB) may exceed WASM heap limits and produce an opaque OOM trap. Neither case is documented or caught with a useful error message. Adding a minimum-length check (e.g. >= 2 bytes for the JFIF/EXIF SOI marker) and an optional maximum-size guard would surface actionable errors before touching WASM.
code_excerpt:
export function decodeJpegScaled(jpeg: Uint8Array, denom: Denom = 1): JpegDecodeResult {
  const r = _decode(jpeg, denom);

title: No CI dependency-audit step for the compiled WASM binary or its Rust crate supply chain
description: The package ships a pre-built WASM binary from `crates/fast-jpeg/pkg/`. There is no evidence of `cargo audit` or `cargo deny` in CI to flag known CVEs in the libjxl/libjpeg-turbo or image-decoding crates that the fast-jpeg crate may transitively pull in. A dependency-audit job (`cargo audit --deny warnings`) would catch supply-chain vulnerabilities automatically.
